Reference: Uzukwu, E. Elochukwu. Memorializing the Unsung: Slaves of the Church and the Making of Kongo Catholicism. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2024.
Note: Stefano Maria da Castelletto, the apostolic prefect for West Central Africa, observed in 1777 that the Capuchins maintained a substantial cohort of four to five hundred slaves in the Soyo mission. Soyo, known as a powerful coastal Catholic province of ancient Kongo, was the preferred headquarters of the Capuchins. This mission served both the Portuguese city of Luanda and, through frequent pastoral visits, the Kingdom of the Kongo, particularly at its capital, Mbanza Kongo-São Salvador. The Capuchin mission lasted from the mid-17th to the early 19th century. Life for slaves within the hospices was highly regimented, with mandatory prayers, exit permits, strict punishments for breaking rules, and the threat of being (re)sold for infractions like theft. However, during pastoral treks, six slaves took turns carrying the priest in a hammock, covering great distances on foot. They kept the fire burning all night, slept under the stars, and protected the priest from threats. Their role, described positively by Capuchin priest Girolamo Merolla da Sorrento, included maintaining the hostel and serving as medical aids in the hospital. Following Kongo-Ndongo-Mbundu social norms, their status was similar to kinless "classificatory children,” dependent on the Capuchin priests as their collective "classificatory father."

Reference: Alden, Dauril. The Making of an Enterprise: The Society of Jesus in Portugal, Its Empire, and Beyond, 1540–1750. Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1996.
Note: In 1760, Jesuits in Angola received donations of slaves from African and Portuguese benefactors. 330 slaves worked at their colegio in Luanda; 750 more worked on the surrounding twenty rural estates (arimos).

Reference: Cushner, Nicholas P. Jesuit Ranches and the Agrarian Development of Colonial Argentina, 1650–1767. Albany: SUNY Press, 1984.
Note: By the first decade of the eighteenth century, 150 Jesuit priests, 100,000 Guaraní, and 1,300 slaves of African descent lived on vast missions in what is now Paraguay. The Jesuits established a vast estancia (cattle ranching) system to fund their missions and procured an enslaved African labor force from the British South Sea Company at Buenos Aires.  By 1767, the order possessed 381 slaves across its properties in Buenos Aires, 1,043 slaves in Córdoba, and 570 in Asunción.

Note: The Jesuits were latecomers to Cuba, but between founding their Colegio San José (in Havana) in 1724 and their expulsion in 1767, they became one of the largest landowners. They acquired plantations (many from benefactors) to support their educational institutions. They were among the six most important sugar producers in Cuba.

Community2: Franciscans (including Poor Clares), Capuchins, Jesuits, Dominicans, Mercedarians, Augustinians, Benedictines
Reference: Maza Miquel, Manuel, S.J. "Clero Católico y Esclavitud en Cuba, Siglos XVI al XIX: Ensayo de Síntesis." Estudios Sociales 79/80 (January–July 1990).
Note: The Catholic Church in Cuba was deeply entangled in the institution of slavery, both as an ideological force and as a direct participant in the enslaved labor economy. Religious institutions, including seminaries, convents, and charitable organizations, financially supported sugar plantations by providing loans and collecting diezmos from their profits. The Convent of Santa Clara, a Franciscan institution, received diezmos from at least 20 sugar plantations during the 18th century, demonstrating the Church’s material dependence on the enslaved labor system. Clergy members and religious organizations also directly owned enslaved people, though the article does not specify exact figures. Additionally, they played a key role in the coartación system, managing funds for enslaved individuals seeking to purchase their freedom. However, by the early 19th century, as Cuba’s sugar economy expanded, wealthy plantation owners began building private chapels and hiring their own priests, reducing the clergy’s influence over enslaved communities. By the mid-19th century, anticlerical reforms and economic modernization further weakened the Church’s role in Cuban society. The Spanish government’s desamortization laws stripped religious orders of much of their property, and the plantation system increasingly marginalized religious instruction for enslaved people. Many plantation owners viewed clergy as meddlesome and restricted their access to enslaved laborers. While some religious figures vocally condemned slavery as early as 1680-1681, their efforts were largely suppressed. Ultimately, the Church remained institutionally complicit in Cuba’s slave economy until abolition in 1886, its influence declining as sugar wealth consolidated in secular hands.

The central Carmelite Convent, located in Guadeloupe, provided each parish with an average of three enslaved workers to serve the priests and maintain church buildings and grounds. The Carmelites also  possessed two sugar plantations on Guadeloupe to support their missions, the oldest at Montagne de Beau Soleil (known as “Mont Carmel”) and another at Belle-Vue, both near the town of Basse-Terre. The Mont Carmel plantation counted 57 enslaved dependents in 1772, predominantly of Igbo descent (but also Bambara, Arada, and Congo).

On of their largest, at Terrier-Rouge, was said to have been worked by 93 enslaved men, women, and children before the Jesuit expulsion.

Reference: Cottineau de Kloguen, Denis Louis. An Historical Sketch of Goa, the Metropolis of the Portuguese Settlements in India. Madras: The Gazette Press, 1831. Noonan, John T. Church That Can and Cannot Change: The Development of Catholic Moral Teaching. Notre Dame: University of Notre Dame Press, 2005.
Note: In addition to paid servants, men and women religious enslaved people of both African and Asian background at their institutions in Goa, capital of Portuguese India. To give a sense of the scope of slaveholding by men and women religious in early Goa, in 1635, the one hundred Augustinian nuns of the Convent of Santa Mônica protested to the local authorities about an ordinance that restricted them to a total of one hundred and twenty slaves. In contrast, any unmarried European in this Portuguese outpost in Asia was allowed to have fifteen or twenty slave girls. The nuns questioned why they should be subject to such limitations. We have slightly clearer estimates for the early nineteenth century. French priest Denis L. Cottineau de Kloguen published An Historical Sketch of Goa at Madras in 1831 based on his experience as a chaplain there; most of Cottineau’s facts, figures, and maps date to around 1827. Setting out to write an accurate account of the history and present state of the city and its surroundings, Cottineau explained that he conversed daily with the bishop at the episcopal palace (his initial residence), conducted interviews with heads of religious institutions, visited monuments, consulted official documents in the archives, and corresponded with the governor of Daman (Cottineau de Kloguen 1831, 3–4). Based on first-hand observations and local sources gathered around 1827, the French missionary Fr. Cottineau estimated that more than 300 “servants and slaves” were attached to the convents, monasteries, hospitals, and clergy of Goa proper. He noted that each priest or senior professed religious was typically allowed an attached servant or slave, while each nun possessed at least one female domestic servant. His figures excluded several religious institutions beyond the city limits, suggesting that the true number of enslaved and dependent laborers serving ecclesiastical institutions in the Goa region was likely considerably higher (85–86). Of the Convent of St. Monica (the Augustinian nuns—the only remaining community of nuns in Goa at the time), Cottineau wrote that the sisters were completely cut off from contact with men apart from clergy, the doctor, “and some old black slaves [who served them] as gardeners” in their cloister (140); as for the "very great" number of female servants, they likely helped the nuns with the making of scapularies and other religious items, as well as fruit preseves and syrups, all of which were sold to benefit the convent (141). More generally, within the complex caste hierarchy of Portuguese India, Cottineau remarked that although Christians predominated, “the slaves of Mozambique may be considered as the last Christian caste” (p. 157). Many of these enslaved and dependent laborers were likely affected by state secularization and confiscation of ecclesiastical property that followed the expulsion of the religious orders in 1835.

Reference: De Sousa, Lúcio. The Portuguese Slave Trade in Early Modern Japan. Leiden: Brill, 2019, 358.
Note: Lúcio de Sousa concludes that the Jesuits possessed around 300 slaves in 1583 and 425 by 1609. Most were young men, and essentially all were domestic servants. The slaves’ origins were mostly Japanese, but also African. Indian, Malay, and Korean, among others. The sites of these slaveholdings were concentrated in Nagasaki (historically the main slaving center for the Portuguese), where the Jesuits had a college, novitiate, and hospital. As de Sousa argues, despite Jesuit apologists’ claims that the priests purchased enslaved Christian children to later release them, the number of slaves held by the Society only grew “as the evangelization endeavor expanded,” right up until the Jesuits’ expulsion from Japan in 1614. One of the reasons the Portuguese were eventually ousted from Japan was their known involvement in trading slaves out of Japan.

Reference: "Inventaire des biens du collège (25 septembre 1784)." Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, F5 A 18. Aix-en-Provence.
Note: The Christian Brothers played a key role in education in Fort-de-France (then Fort-Royal) during the 18th century. Two major institutions, the Collège de Saint-Victor for boys (established in 1768) and the Maison de la Providence for girls (established in 1763), were created under the influence of Father Charles François. These schools offered different curriculums: boys received education in practical subjects like mathematics, geography, and military sciences, while girls were taught virtues, domestic skills, and basic literacy. Both institutions faced challenges and eventually closed during the French Revolution.

Reference: Konrad, Herman W. A Jesuit Hacienda in Colonial Mexico: Santa Lucía, 1576–1767. Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1980. Riley, James D. "The Wealth of the Jesuits in Mexico, 1670–1767." The Americas 33, no. 2 (1976): 226–266.
Note: In 1767, some 7,000 enslaved men, women, and children worked on the Jesuits’ ranches and 122 haciendas throughout New Spain, which generated funds to support their colleges, missions, and other enterprises. The first, largest, and most profitable of these haciendas was Santa Lucia, established to provide revenues for the order’s college in Mexico City. The article "The Wealth of the Jesuits in Mexico, 1670-1767" examines the economic activities of the Jesuits, highlighting their efficient management of haciendas and obrajes, despite facing financial challenges. It notes that Jesuit-managed obrajes were associated with harsh labor conditions, reflecting broader issues of labor exploitation in colonial New Spain. The Jesuits' economic success was due in part to tax exemptions and meticulous financial oversight, though ethical concerns about their labor practices were prevalent.

Location: San Francisco Xavier de la Nasca
Location2: San Francisco Xavier de la Nasca
Reference: Weaver, Brendan J. M. "'The Grace of God and Virtue of Obedience': The Archaeology of Slavery and the Jesuit Hacienda Systems of Nasca, Peru, 1619–1767." Journal of Jesuit Studies 8, no. 3 (2021): 430–53.
Note: In the Nazca region of Spanish-colonial Peru, Jesuit missionaries owned large vineyards. The hacienda San Joseph de la Nasca, situated in the upper part of the middle Ingenio Valley, belonged to the Jesuit College of Cuzco, while San Francisco Xavier de la Nasca, in the lower part of the same valley, was owned by the Jesuit College of San Pablo in Lima. Both estates relied heavily on enslaved workers of sub-Saharan African descent, who lived under a highly regimented system of Christian discipline. These enslaved communities were diverse, cosmopolitan, and multilingual, with individuals from various regions of West and Central Africa. In addition to producing wine and brandy, the estates had substantial facilities for manufacturing botijas, ceramic storage jars used for transporting their products. Archaeological evidence reveals that despite the coercive labor structure, enslaved individuals asserted cultural agency through self-provisioning activities and the incorporation of African traditions into their daily lives, including the use of garden plots and self-raised livestock. In 1767, crown inventories recorded 278 enslaved individuals at San Joseph and 306 at San Xavier. The estates were part of the larger holdings of the Society of Jesus, who, overall, owned a total of 5,224 enslaved individuals across their properties in Peru.

Community2: Order of Hermits of Saint Augustine - Province of Portugal
Reference: Vieira, Domingos, and Carlos A. Moreira Azevedo. Ordem dos eremitas de Santo Agostinho em Portugal (1256–1834). História religiosa 8. Lisboa: Universidade Católica Portuguesa, Centro de Estudos de História Religiosa, 2011.

Note: “Substantial numbers of enslaved people lived in the cosmopolitan European port cities like Lisbon, Seville, and Rome where the Jesuits established themselves. The slave population was diverse, including Muslims and moriscos (Muslims who converted to Christianity and their descendants), North Africans and sub-Saharan Africans, and indios (indigenous people) trafficked from the Americas and Asia. … It would not be surprising to find enslaved people ministered to by Jesuits and working at Jesuit colleges and schools in southern Europe from the earliest days of the Society.”

Reference: Poole, Stafford, and Douglas J. Slawson. Church and Slave in Perry County, Missouri, 1818–1865. Studies in American Religion, vol. 22. Lewiston, NY: E. Mellen Press, 1986. "Charles, a Man of Color v. Peter Verhagen." http://repository.wustl.edu/concern/texts/hx11xg50c.
Note: Louis Dubourg (vicar-apostolic of the Louisiana Territory) and the first bishop of St. Louis, Joseph Rosati bought and sold slaves on behalf of the priests of the Congregation of the Mission (known as Vincentians, or Lazarists) for their seminary at Perryville, Missouri (called St. Mary's of the Barrens). Founded in 1817, it was the first US institution of higher learning west of the Mississippi River.  By the taking of the 1830 federal census, twenty-seven enslaved men, women, and children resided at the seminary, around twenty of whom the priests claimed to own outright. In 1840, Charles, who, at various points in his life, served both the seminary and Bishop Rosati directly, successfully sued for his freedom. According to the court records, Charles had been brought by Rosati to work on a construction project (a new convent building for the Sisters of St. Joseph) across the river in Illinois, free territory.

Community: Religious of the Sacred Heart of Jesus
Community2: Sisters of the Society of the Sacred Heart
Reference: Croxall, Christine. "Teaching Authority: The Sacred Heart Sisters' Mississippi River Valley Mission, 1818–30." "Old St. Ferdinand Shrine." https://www.oldstferdinandshrine.com/about-the-shrine/.
Note: "Along with bishops, priests, the Jesuits and most of the families of their students, the communities of the Religious of the Sacred Heart, from the time of Philippine [Duchesne] until the Civil War, owned, bought and sold enslaved persons in the slave states of Missouri and Louisiana. Enslaved persons built the buildings, made the bricks and sustained the foundations. They worked side by side with the sisters, taking care of the children, cooking, washing and gardening. ... It is known that the Society of the Sacred Heart had enslaved persons in Grand Coteau, Louisiana; Natchitoches, Louisiana; Convent, Louisiana; and St. Louis and Florissant, Missouri."... In September 2018, the Academy of the Sacred Heart in Grand Coteau dedicated "a monument in the parish cemetery naming the enslaved persons of the convent known to be buried there. The museum at the school will have an area dedicated to the convent’s history and acknowledgment of its role in slavery. The names of all known enslaved persons will be part of this area of the museum. A plaque will be placed at the slave quarters naming those living there in its first years."[9] "The Society of the Sacred Heart announced the creation of the Cor Unum Scholarship to provide tuition assistance to African American students desiring a Sacred Heart education at Schools of the Sacred Heart – Grand Coteau in Grand Coteau, Louisiana, and to provide professional development for faculty and staff and/or course curriculum to students on inclusion and diversity." Society of the Sacred Heart United States – Canada. https://rscj.org/next-steps. Accessed October 15, 2019.

Community: Sisters of Charity of St. Joseph
Reference: Morrow, Diane Batts. Persons of Color and Religious at the Same Time: The Oblate Sisters of Providence, 1828–1860. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2002, 184.
Note: Father Louis Regis Deluol, P.S.S. (superior of the U.S. Sulpicians from 1829-1849) wrote to Sister Margaret George, treasurer of the Sisters of Charity of St. Joseph at Emmitsburg, Maryland, about the buying and selling of that community's enslaved residents. He referred to two mixed-race enslaved residents of Emmitsburg as "yellow boys."

Reference: Pyatt, Susanna. "Federal Census Evidence for Loretto Slaveholding, 1820–1860." Loretto Community (blog), November 18, 2020. https://www.lorettocommunity.org/federal-census-evidence-for-loretto-slaveholding-1820-1860/.
Note: Founded in 1812, the Sisters of Loretto were established in Kentucky by the Belgian missionary priest, Charles Nerinckx (original name: The Little Society of the Friends of Mary at the Foot of the Cross). Their missions included the education of children on the frontier, and they were among the first Catholic groups to admit notices of African descent, although they were segregated in living spaces. The Sisters collaborated closely with local Jesuits, based at St. Louis, including in their mission work to American Indians, but also in the exchanging and renting of enslaved people. Like the other important communities of women religious in the American South and West, such as the Ursulines and Sacred Heart Sisters, the Sisters of Loretto also owned slaves brought to their convents as part of the dowries of white novices (many of whom, in the case of the Loretto sisters, hailed from Maryland settler families). According to federal census data, enslaved people resided on the expanding properties (schools and residences) of the Loretto community’s academies in Kentucky and Missouri at least from 1820 through 1860. 23 were registered as living at the Motherhouse in Little Loretto (near St. Mary’s, Kentucky), in 1850.

Community2: Society of the Priests of Saint Sulpice
Reference: Ulshafer, Thomas R. "Slavery and the Early Sulpician Community in Maryland." U.S. Catholic Historian 37, no. 2 (2019): 1–21. https://doi.org/10.1353/cht.2019.0013.
Note: The Sulpicians established a second seminary and farm at Emmitsburg, Maryland (site of today’s Mount St. Mary's University), which remained in the hands of the society from 1809 to 1826. 1820 census data lists sixteen bondspeople on the property by that year, under the name of the superior of St. Mary’s seminary, Father John Dubois (1764-1822), future bishop of New York, who had personally purchased the land and some of the enslaved laborers at Emmitsburg.

Reference: Clark, Emily. Masterless Mistresses: The New Orleans Ursulines and the Development of a New World Society, 1727–1834. Williamsburg, VA: Omohundro Institute of Early American History and Culture; Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2007, 169.
Note: In 1770, the plantation operated by the French, and French- and Spanish-Creole Ursuline nuns of New Orleans was home to 61 slaves; in 1791, 17 slaves were censused as living in the convent itself (24 in 1795).
